title: Failure--Assumption Reconstruction: A Two-Level Protocol for Diagnosing and Repairing Failed Approaches
author: Esposito, Giovanni
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Failed approaches are rarely isolated propositions. In serious inquiry, a route that fails typically consists of a target claim together with an assumption bundle, a witness settlement, an evidential posture, and a distribution of participation cost that is often left implicit. Existing traditions address important parts of this problem. Duhem–Quine explains why hypotheses are not tested in isolation. Lakatos characterizes progressive and degenerating revision trajectories. Severe-testing traditions distinguish weak from strong evidence of failure. Argumentation-based frameworks clarify admissibility among competing assumption sets. Yet, to our knowledge, no prior operational protocol combines these elements while also treating witness-cost as a first-class object. 
 This paper introduces the Failure–Assumption Reconstruction Protocol (FARP), a two-level protocol for diagnosing and repairing failed approaches. At Level 1, FARP reconstructs failed object-level routes as typed bundles consisting of target claim, assumption family, witness-cost profile, failure surface, and evidence of failure. At Level 2, FARP reconstructs the routing policy by which such failures are interpreted, repaired, remembered, and generalized. The protocol is governed by three principles: Bundle Structure, Shared Failure Cores, and Witness Settlement. It also separates object failure, certificate failure, and witness-cost distortion, preventing category conflation. A verifier PASS is treated as structural integrity only unless an explicit correctness witness is supplied. 
 The paper argues that failure need not be treated either as a dead end or as a license for unconstrained improvisation. Under typed evidence, admissibility discipline, and explicit witness accounting, failed approaches can become structured evidence for what to retain, what to replace, what to block from silent re-entry, and what kind of next move is now lawfully available. The manuscript includes a witness-cost necessity test, robustness and novelty tests, a negative-result calibration module, a compact signature-library account, and a discussion of bounded-recursive coordination as a lens on routing failure.

keywords: failure reconstruction",     "witness-cost",     "philosophy of science",     "scientific methodology",     "underdetermination",     "Lakatos",     "Duhem-Quine",     "epistemic labor",     "negative-result calibration"
